content.text: Compute is the foundational AWS service category — it covers every way you can run code in the cloud. AWS offers a broad spectrum from raw virtual machines where you control everything, to fully serverless platforms where you only supply the code. Choosing the right compute service is one of the most consequential architecture decisions you make, affecting cost, operational overhead, scalability, and developer experience.

attrs.alt: AWS compute service landscape showing EC2, Lambda, ECS/EKS, Elastic Beanstalk, Fargate, Batch, App Runner, and Lightsail, plus an abstraction spectrum from low (EC2) to high (App Runner)

content.text: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is the foundational compute service. It provisions virtual servers — called instances — from a catalog of over 750 instance types spanning CPUs, GPUs, memory-optimized, and storage-optimized configurations. You control the operating system, networking, storage, and software stack.

— General purpose (t, m), Compute optimized (c), Memory optimized (r, x), Storage optimized (d, i), GPU (p, g), Inferentia (inf). Each letter denotes the family; the number is the generation; the suffix (large, xlarge, 2xlarge) is the size within the family.

— On-Demand (pay by the hour/second, no commitment), Reserved (1 or 3 year commitment, up to 72% discount), Spot (spare capacity at up to 90% discount but can be interrupted with 2-minute notice), Savings Plans (flexible discount applied to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda spend).

— Amazon Machine Images are the templates for an instance. AWS provides AMIs for Amazon Linux 2023, Ubuntu, Windows Server, RHEL, and more. You can create custom AMIs from a running instance to bake in your software and configuration.
— a shell script (or cloud-init config) that runs once at instance launch, used to install packages or apply configuration automatically.

Lambda runs code in response to events without any servers to provision or manage. You upload a function (in Python, Node.js, Java, Go, Ruby, .NET, or a custom runtime), configure triggers, and AWS handles execution, scaling, and availability. Billing is per-invocation and per-GB-second of memory × duration.
— API Gateway (HTTP), S3 events (file upload), DynamoDB Streams, SQS/SNS, EventBridge, Kinesis, Cognito, and more. Lambda is the glue that connects AWS services in event-driven architectures.

— each invocation gets an isolated runtime environment. AWS reuses warm containers to avoid cold start latency; the first request after a period of inactivity incurs a cold start (tens to hundreds of milliseconds depending on runtime and package size).
— 15 minute maximum execution time, 10 GB maximum memory, 10 GB ephemeral /tmp storage (configurable up to 10 GB), 1,000 concurrent executions by default (can be increased).
— 1 million free invocations and 400,000 GB-seconds of compute time per month. Lambda is effectively free at low volumes.

# Lambda handler — processes an S3 event
import boto3, json

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

def handler(event, context):
    for record in event['Records']:
        bucket = record['s3']['bucket']['name']
        key = record['s3']['object']['key']
        obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        data = json.loads(obj['Body'].read())
        print(f"Processed {key}: {len(data)} records")
    return {'statusCode': 200}

ECS is AWS's native container orchestration service. You define tasks (one or more Docker containers with their CPU, memory, and port config), group them into services (which maintain a desired count and integrate with load balancers), and run them on either EC2 instances you manage or Fargate (serverless).
— a JSON blueprint declaring container image, CPU/memory, port mappings, environment variables, IAM task role, and logging config (typically to CloudWatch Logs via awslogs driver).
— maintains a desired number of running task copies, integrates with an ALB target group for load balancing, and replaces unhealthy tasks automatically.
— EC2 (you manage the underlying instances and cluster capacity) or Fargate (AWS manages the infrastructure; you pay per vCPU-second and GB-second of the task).
— Elastic Container Registry is the companion service for storing Docker images privately in AWS, with vulnerability scanning and lifecycle policies.

EKS runs a managed Kubernetes control plane. AWS owns and operates the etcd cluster and API servers across three availability zones; you run worker nodes (EC2 node groups or Fargate profiles) and deploy workloads using standard Kubernetes manifests. EKS is the right choice when your team already knows Kubernetes or needs the full Kubernetes ecosystem (Helm, Argo CD, Istio, etc.).
— AWS provisions EC2 worker nodes in an Auto Scaling Group. OS patching and node replacement during Kubernetes version upgrades are handled for you.
— run pods without managing worker nodes at all; pods matching a namespace/label selector are scheduled onto Fargate.
— run EKS on your own on-premises hardware using the same APIs and tooling. Useful for hybrid and edge scenarios.
— $0.10/hour per cluster for the control plane, plus EC2 or Fargate charges for worker nodes.

Fargate is the serverless compute engine for ECS and EKS. Instead of provisioning and managing EC2 instances, you specify the CPU and memory for each task or pod and AWS handles the rest — patching, scaling capacity, and running the underlying hardware. You are billed per vCPU-second and GB-second of memory while the task runs.
— bursty or unpredictable workloads, teams that want container-level abstractions without cluster management, and microservice deployments where tasks start and stop frequently.
— sustained high-throughput workloads where Reserved Instance savings offset Fargate's per-task cost, or when you need specific instance features (GPU, very large memory, instance-store NVMe).
AWS Elastic Beanstalk — Platform as a Service

Elastic Beanstalk is AWS's PaaS offering. You upload your application code (or a container image), select a platform (Python, Node.js, Java, PHP, Ruby, Go, .NET, Docker), and Beanstalk automatically provisions an EC2 Auto Scaling Group, an Application Load Balancer, CloudWatch alarms, and an S3 bucket for deployment artifacts. It removes infrastructure configuration entirely from the developer's view.
— each application has one or more environments (e.g., production, staging). Beanstalk supports blue/green deployments by swapping CNAME between two environments.
— Beanstalk itself is free. You pay for the EC2 instances, load balancer, and RDS database (if attached) at normal rates.
— fast deployment for standard web applications where the team doesn't want to learn ECS/EKS but needs more control than App Runner. Best for teams migrating from Heroku or similar PaaS.

App Runner is AWS's highest-abstraction compute option for web applications and APIs. You point it at a source code repository (GitHub) or a container image, configure a few settings (port, CPU, memory), and App Runner builds, deploys, and scales the application automatically. There are no clusters, task definitions, or load balancers to configure.

App Runner scales to zero when idle and back up on demand, making it cost-effective for low-traffic APIs. Unlike Lambda, it can run long-duration requests without the 15-minute constraint, making it suitable for synchronous APIs that hit databases or external services.

AWS Batch is purpose-built for batch computing — work that can be parallelized across many workers, runs to completion, and does not require continuous availability. It manages a queue of jobs, spins up EC2 or Fargate compute environments, runs the jobs, and terminates the compute when done. Use cases include genomics processing, financial risk modeling, rendering, and ML training.
— jobs are submitted to a queue with a priority. Multiple queues can target the same compute environment (e.g., high-priority vs low-priority queues sharing Spot capacity).
— defines the EC2 or Fargate capacity. EC2 compute environments can use Spot Instances for up to 90% cost reduction on fault-tolerant batch jobs.
— jobs can declare dependencies on other jobs, enabling pipeline-style processing where job B starts only after job A succeeds.

Lightsail provides simple virtual private servers at predictable monthly prices ($3.50–$160/month). It bundles compute, storage, and a data transfer allowance into a single fixed price, hiding the complexity of EC2 instance types, EBS volumes, and data transfer charges. It is designed for small websites, WordPress blogs, development environments, and simple APIs where simplicity matters more than fine-grained control.
Lightsail instances run on the same hardware as EC2 and can be peered with a VPC for access to full AWS services when needed.
Full OS control, custom AMI, GPU
Maximum flexibility at the cost of management
Upload code, want AWS to do everything
Beanstalk: more config; App Runner: zero config
Parallel batch jobs, HPC, ML training
Regardless of which compute service you choose, several cross-cutting concerns apply the same way across the entire category:
— every compute service (EC2 instance profile, Lambda execution role, ECS task role, EKS pod identity) assumes an IAM role for AWS API access. Never embed access keys in compute environments.
— EC2, ECS tasks, EKS pods, Lambda (with VPC config), and Batch jobs can all be placed in VPC subnets, subject to security group rules.
— stdout and stderr from all compute types (Lambda, ECS, EKS, Beanstalk) flows to CloudWatch Logs when configured. Set log retention periods to avoid unbounded storage cost.
— EC2 uses Auto Scaling Groups; ECS uses Application Auto Scaling on service desired count; Lambda and Fargate scale automatically; EKS uses Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter.
— apply consistent tags (env, team, project, cost-center) to EC2 instances, ECS services, and Lambda functions for cost allocation reports in AWS Cost Explorer.

Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs) are the operational backbone of EC2 workloads in production. An ASG maintains a desired number of healthy instances, replacing failed ones and adjusting capacity based on demand. Most production EC2 deployments live inside an ASG rather than running individual instances directly.
— defines the AMI, instance type, security groups, IAM profile, and user data for ASG instances. Replacing the launch template version triggers a rolling instance refresh.

— Target Tracking (maintain a metric like 60% CPU), Step Scaling (increase by N when threshold breached by M), or Scheduled Scaling (set capacity at specific times for predictable load). Target Tracking is the recommended default.

— an ASG can run multiple instance types (e.g., m5.large, m5a.large, m6i.large) across On-Demand and Spot capacity with configurable ratios. This maximizes Spot availability while keeping a guaranteed On-Demand baseline.
— by default, ASGs use EC2 health (instance status checks). Add ALB health check integration so unhealthy application targets are replaced, not just failed instances.

Understanding Lambda's execution model is essential for building reliable serverless applications. Lambda scales by running multiple concurrent instances of your function — not by running a single instance faster.

— each in-flight invocation uses one concurrency unit. An account starts with a 1,000 concurrency limit (shared across all functions in the region). Reserve concurrency for critical functions to guarantee their share.

— when Lambda needs a new execution environment, it initializes the runtime and runs your init code (code outside the handler). This adds latency. Minimize init time by keeping packages small and moving SDK clients outside the handler.
— pre-warms a set number of execution environments to eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive APIs. You pay for the idle pre-warmed environments even when no invocations occur.

— available for Java runtimes. AWS takes a snapshot of an initialized execution environment and restores from the snapshot instead of re-initializing, reducing Java cold starts from seconds to milliseconds.

# Correct: initialize clients outside the handler so they are reused across warm invocations
import boto3

# This runs during init (cold start) and is cached for warm invocations
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table('users')

def handler(event, context):
    # This runs on every invocation but skips the SDK initialization cost
    user_id = event['userId']
    item = table.get_item(Key={'userId': user_id})
    return item.get('Item')

Lambda supports container images up to 10 GB, enabling use cases that don't fit in a deployment ZIP (large ML models, complex native dependencies, large binaries). The container must implement the Lambda Runtime Interface, which AWS base images handle automatically.

# Dockerfile for a Lambda container image
FROM public.ecr.aws/lambda/python:3.12

# Copy requirements and install
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

# Copy function code
COPY app.py .

# Lambda handler: file.function_name
CMD ["app.handler"]

Build and push to ECR, then reference the image URI in your Lambda function config. This workflow unifies container tooling (Dockerfile, ecr, docker build) with Lambda's serverless execution model — teams already using ECS can apply the same build pipeline to Lambda.
Compute costs in AWS are shaped by the billing model. Understanding the fundamental unit of billing for each service prevents surprise bills and guides architectural tradeoffs.

At high sustained utilization (>70% of the hour), EC2 Reserved Instances are almost always cheaper than Lambda or Fargate for the same amount of compute. Lambda and Fargate win when workloads are bursty or infrequent — you pay nothing for idle time.
IMDS is an HTTP endpoint available inside every EC2 instance at
. It provides instance identity, placement, and temporary credentials from the attached IAM instance profile — no network call outside the host required.

# IMDSv2 (required on newer instances — uses a session token for SSRF protection)
TOKEN=$(curl -X PUT http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token \
  -H 'X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600')

# Get instance ID
curl -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" \
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id

# Get availability zone
curl -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" \
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/placement/availability-zone

# Get IAM role credentials (boto3/SDK does this automatically)
curl -H "X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: $TOKEN" \
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/MyRole
IMDSv2 (token-based) is required for security; IMDSv1 (no token) is vulnerable to SSRF attacks. Enforce IMDSv2 in your launch templates by setting
and block IMDSv1 at the organization level via an SCP.

AWS Graviton processors are AWS-designed Arm-based CPUs that offer better price/performance than x86 equivalents for many workloads. Graviton3 (current generation, 2022) powers the C7g, M7g, R7g, and other 'g' suffix instance families.
— Graviton instances are typically 20% cheaper than the equivalent x86 instance at the same performance level, or 40% better performance per dollar.

— any language that runs on Linux/Arm64 works: Python, Node.js, Java, Go, Rust, Ruby. Native binaries compiled for x86 require recompilation (or emulation, which negates the benefit). Most Docker images have multi-arch support or separate arm64 tags.
— Lambda supports arm64 architecture for up to 34% better price/performance. Enable with a one-line config change and rebuild your deployment package for arm64.
— mixed architecture node groups let you run x86 and arm64 pods in the same cluster; use node affinity rules to schedule pods on the correct architecture.

# Lambda with arm64 architecture (Graviton)
resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" {
  function_name = "my-api"
  runtime       = "python3.12"
  architectures = ["arm64"]   # Enable Graviton
  handler       = "app.handler"
  filename      = "function.zip"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  memory_size   = 512
  timeout       = 30
}
AWS extends compute to the network edge, enabling code execution close to end users or IoT devices without a round trip to a central region.

— ultra-lightweight JavaScript functions that execute at CloudFront edge locations for every request. Sub-millisecond latency, limited to URL rewrites, header manipulation, and simple redirects. 10 million free invocations per month.

— full Lambda functions (Node.js or Python) deployed to CloudFront edge locations. Runs on origin request/response and viewer request/response lifecycle events. Supports async calls and larger payloads than CloudFront Functions. Billed like standard Lambda but at a 3× higher per-GB-second rate.
— deploys EC2 and ECS inside 5G carrier networks (Verizon, Vodafone, KDDI) for single-digit millisecond latency to 5G devices. Used for gaming, AR/VR, and autonomous vehicle applications.
— full AWS compute on your on-premises rack. Runs EC2, ECS, EKS, and RDS with the same AWS APIs, managed by AWS but physically in your data center.

AWS compute ranges from EC2 (full VM control) to Lambda (pure event-driven functions) to App Runner (zero-config web apps); the right choice depends on how much control vs. managed convenience your team needs.
ECS is AWS-native container orchestration; EKS is managed Kubernetes. Both can use Fargate to remove the need to manage worker nodes.
Lambda is billed per invocation and per GB-second of execution; it is free at low volumes and scales instantly to handle spiky, event-driven traffic.
All compute services share the same IAM, VPC, CloudWatch, and billing infrastructure — learning these fundamentals once applies across the entire category.
EC2 Reserved Instances and Savings Plans provide the largest discounts (up to 72%) for predictable baseline compute; Spot Instances provide up to 90% discounts for fault-tolerant workloads.
